description: Create a three-column content slide titled 'We recommend' with recommendation bullet lists on youth marketing, smartphone-independent services, cultural relevance, permission space, and parent-friendly propositions.

items.text: If reaching younger people matters to a brand, it will be crucial to workshop a less digital/social media-dependent strategy.
items.sub: Alternatives include putting retail (and retail media) in the mix, going old school with on-the-ground promotion in shopping malls, a greater weighting towards events on television and streaming services, and sponsorship.

items.text: If limits on child- or teen-focused apps and devices are imposed, it will be necessary to redesign or create services that don’t depend on smartphones.
items.sub: Alternatively, experiences can bypass controversial content domains or be ring-fenced in more content-limited interfaces. Unbundling the smartphone creates opportunity.

items.text: Organizations should ensure they stay on top of what is culturally relevant.
items.sub: Social media continues to be the forge and main distribution channel for ideas, language shifts and fashion. Likely this won’t change for those over 16, but movements that originate or are perpetuated in non-digital ways may become more important.

items.text: It’s important to think through an organization’s permission space.
items.sub: Will using social media advertising/commerce to engage with young people be acceptable for the company, or will it come under attack from concerned parties? And of course, if the brand serves as any kind of gateway to harmful content, whether generally or specifically to either boys or girls, it must be dealt with—immediately and robustly.

items.text: If parents’ gatekeeping role for children and young people expands, brands should consider how to create parent-friendly propositions
items.sub: that help build an affinity with wary parents and create relevance for their children.
